subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Rawâdata tables include uncertainties and units, showing awareness of measurement error
Graphs are clearly labelled with axes titles and units
Percentage uncertainty formula is communicated accurately with correct notation
weaknesses: Time units are omitted from some table headers
No calculation of means, standard deviations or flow rates
Error bars are uniform rather than based on calculated propagation
Uncertainty propagation into processed data or trendlines is absent
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Data communication is generally clear, and the student correctly quotes uncertainties and presents error analysis formulae. However, significant omissions in data processing (no averaging or error propagation into graphs) and inconsistencies in unit labelling limit the work to the midâband. A score of 3 appropriately reflects competent but incomplete analysis.

strengths: Clear, specific research question linked to an appropriate industrial context
Detailed, numbered procedure and comprehensive materials list support reproducibility
Thorough identification and justification of controlled variables
Effective risk assessment identifying hazards and mitigation strategies
weaknesses: Some procedural instructions are ambiguous (e.g., âopen until maximum outputâ)
Background theory lacks quantitative linkage and some citations lack full bibliographic detail
Units are omitted in parts of the variables table
Volume measurement ambiguity (â25 ml or 25 dropsâ)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The design clearly moves beyond a broad outline: the research question is situated in a defined industrial context and the procedure includes precise distances, angles and volumes allowing reproducibility. Controlled variables are well justified and safety aspects are addressed. However, minor ambiguities (volume specification, start/stop cues, missing units) prevent it reaching full marks in the top band. A score of 5 reflects the high overall quality with room for more rigorous detail.

strengths: Conclusion accurately reflects the observed trend that steeper funnels decrease flow time
Links findings to accepted scientific context (boundaryâlayer theory, viscosity, gravity)
weaknesses: Conclusion is qualitative and does not integrate quantitative uncertainties or effect sizes
Explanations of context lack depth and citation of quantitative models
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is justified by the data and consistent with the analysis, and the student makes relevant comparisons to fluidâdynamics theory, moving beyond a superficial statement. The absence of quantified uncertainties and deeper theoretical integration keeps this at the upper midâband. A score of 4 reflects a wellâsupported but improvable conclusion.

strengths: Identifies specific methodological limitations (angle measurement, reaction time, funnel stability)
Proposes realistic and relevant improvements linked to each weakness
weaknesses: Does not explain the relative impact or magnitude of improvements on uncertainty reduction
Extension suggestions (e.g., temperature variation) lack detailed design plans
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation shows reflective analysis by describing concrete limitations and corresponding realistic improvements. The lack of explanation of how much each change would improve reliability and the superficial extension design prevent full marks. A score of 4 accurately captures a strong descriptive evaluation with room for more detailed impact analysis.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research question is clearly stated and linked to a defined practical situation (filling bottles, industrial transfer of reactive liquids).  The student explains why efficiency matters and explicitly lists the tilt angles to be studied.  While the everyday context is not deeply elaborated, it is specific enough to move beyond a merely âbroadâ outline, yet not exceptionally rich.  This fits best with the upper end of the 3â4 band.

Uncertainties in angle (Â±0.5Â°) and time (Â±10 s) are quoted and percentage uncertainties for every trial are calculated, showing definite attention to error.  However, the chosen Â±10 s far exceeds reaction-time scale, propagation into means/trendlines is absent, and uncertainty bars in graphs appear arbitrary.  This demonstrates consideration but with notable omissions, matching the 3â4 band.

Independent, dependent and four controlled variables are identified and briefly justified, showing an understanding of relevance.  However, explanations are short; sufficiency of data (only five angles, three trials) is asserted but not justified.  Therefore the methodological considerations are described rather than fully explained, meriting the lower half of the 3â4 band.

Specific weaknesses are identified (angle measurement accuracy, reaction-time error, funnel shift, flow-rate control) and briefly linked to their likely effects, though the magnitude or relative impact is not analysed in detail.  Hence the strand is best described rather than fully explained, earning a score in the 3â4 band.

A numbered procedure, distances, volumes and angles are provided so a replication is possible, but several ambiguities remain (e.g., âopen until maximum output,â unclear start/stop timing cues, no specification of liquid supply height control, or how â25 dropsâ alternative is implemented).  These minor gaps place the work in the middle of the 3â4 band rather than the top.

Processing is limited to plotting raw times and computing simple percentage uncertainties; means, standard deviations, rates of flow, or comparative calculations are not presented.  Some uncertainty values are inconsistent with the formula shown.  These weaknesses constitute significant omissions, placing the work in the upper end of the 1â2 band.

Improvements such as using an angle finder, high-speed camera, consistent drop rate and securing the funnel are realistic and relevant, and each is briefly connected to the associated weakness.  Explanation of how much each would improve reliability is lacking, so the work sits solidly in the descriptive level of the 3â4 band.

Raw-data tables include titles, units and stated instrumental uncertainties, and graphs are labelled, so communication is generally clear.  Precision is weaker: some headings omit units (seconds), error bars look uniform rather than calculated.  Clarity is acceptable, precision inconsistent, so the strand fits the 3â4 band with a mid-score.

The student links findings to boundary-layer theory, no-slip condition, viscosity values from literature and gravity.  These references constitute a relevant comparison to accepted context, yet explanations are brief and lack critical depth or citation of quantitative models.  This corresponds to the mid-range of the 3â4 band.

The stated conclusion (steeper funnels give faster flow, viscosity matters) matches the general trend in the data and references specific observations (e.g., syrup vs water).  Nonetheless, it is qualitative and does not integrate uncertainty or quantitative comparison, so it is âdescribed but not fully consistent,â aligning with the 3â4 band.

annotations.comment: The hypothesis is concise and logically follows from the background principles.
The viscosity definition is clear and wellâsupported by a reliable encyclopaedia source.
The controlled variables table demonstrates thorough identification of factors to mitigate error.
The title clearly identifies the independent and dependent variables, providing a concise focus for the investigation.
The citation â(Boundary Layer, n.d.)â lacks sufficient bibliographic detail; include author or organization and date.
The title could be enhanced by specifying the types of liquids used to clarify the scope of the experiment.
The introduction situates the experiment in everyday and industrial contexts, effectively justifying the practical relevance.
The research question phrase â25 ml or 25 dropsâ is ambiguous. Choose one consistent volume measurement to avoid confusion.
The boundary-layer description is accurate and demonstrates understanding of no-slip flow near surfaces.
The background lacks quantitative linkage between funnel angle and flow rate; including an equation (e.g. HagenâPoiseuille) would strengthen the theoretical context.
The gravity section is superficial and relies on an unclear citation âNordtvedt et al., 2025â; consider deeper fluidâdynamics explanation.
The variables table omits units for the time measurement; specify âsâ for seconds in the dependent variable apparatus table.
The risk assessment identifies stability hazards and appropriate clamp usage to prevent funnel collapse.
Disposing oil and syrup via running water may violate environmental protocols; propose recycling or waste containers.
Rawâdata tables present uncertainties for angle and time, demonstrating awareness of measurement error.
Time units (âsâ) are omitted from raw data headers; include them to enhance clarity and precision.
No calculation of average flow time across trials is shown; include means and standard deviations to improve data processing quality.
Figure 1 is labeled clearly with axes titles and units, making data communication effective.
The placementâofâdroplet control lacks a specific distance value in the table; include the exact length for reproducibility.
The stepwise procedure is detailed and supports reproducibility of the experiment.
Instruction âopen until maximum outputâ is ambiguous; specify fluid flow rate or valve position to standardize start conditions.
The materials list is comprehensive, covering all chemicals and apparatus needed for the investigation.
The chemical listing omits purity or viscosity data for oil and syrup; include these specifications for experimental clarity.
Error bars on graphs appear uniform rather than based on calculated propagation; recalculate uncertainties for each point.
The percentageâuncertainty formula is clearly stated using $$â¦$$ notation, reflecting proper mathematical communication.
Uncertainty propagation into mean values or trendlines is absent; include combined uncertainties when reporting processed data.
The conclusion accurately reflects the observed trend that steeper funnels decrease flow time.
The conclusion does not integrate quantitative uncertainties or effect sizes; include error margins to justify consistency with analysis.
The evaluation identifies specific methodological limitations, demonstrating reflective analysis of weaknesses.
Improvements are relevant but lack discussion of how much each will reduce measurement uncertainty; explain relative impact.
The extension proposes creative factors like temperature but lacks a detailed design plan for varying temperature.
The bibliography includes diverse sources but citation formatting is inconsistent, missing author names or publication dates in some entries.

rubricData.name: Physics IA
rubricData.rubricSpecificInfo: âThe Physics Internal Assessment (IA) is a critical component of the IB Diploma Programme, providing students with the opportunity to design and conduct independent investigations into physics phenomena. This assessment emphasizes the development of practical skills, analytical thinking, and a deep understanding of scientific methodologies. By engaging in the IA, students not only apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios but also cultivate competencies in research design, data analysis, and critical evaluation, all of which are essential for academic and professional success in scientific fields. â
